topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy flyer, tucked into the school newsletter, felt like a physical slap across my face. Sarah Jenkins. My daughter, Lily, was in Mrs. Jenkins’ second-grade class. And now, my son, Tom, was in the same room. A slow, tight knot began to form in my stomach, a cold, prickly sensation that spread outward. I watched Lily’s face as she recounted a story about a caterpillar, noting the familiar, warm crinkles around Mrs. Jenkins’ eyes. It was an effortless connection, a shared history I wasn't privy to. I started hovering near the classroom door during drop-off, feigning interest in the bulletin board, my jaw muscles clenching so hard I could feel the strain in my temples. Every time Mrs. Jenkins offered a cheerful greeting, a small, involuntary tightening occurred in my chest, a sharp, unwelcome pang of comparison.

[story 2]

I found the information buried deep in an old school directory, the paper trembling slightly in my hand. Tom. My son. Mrs. Albright. The realization hit me with the force of a physical blow. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, making my skin feel too tight. I spent the afternoon staring out the window, the sunlight feeling abrasive against my eyes. When I finally spoke to my husband, my voice came out unnaturally high, brittle. "She taught both of us?" I asked, the question sounding less like an inquiry and more like an accusation. I found myself meticulously rearranging the spice rack in the kitchen, every jar aligned with obsessive precision, a desperate attempt to impose some order on the swirling, unsettling feeling inside me.

[story 3]

It was the casual mention during a PTA meeting that shattered my composure. "Oh, Mrs. Davies, she's wonderful. She taught Lily and Tom in the third grade." The words hung in the air, heavy and undeniable. I forced a polite smile, the muscles in my face feeling stiff and unfamiliar. Internally, a strange, restless energy surged through me, making my hands twitch against my thighs under the table. I focused intently on my water glass, watching the ripples distort the light. I started reviewing old photographs of Lily and Tom, scrutinizing every shared glance, searching for some hidden marker, some indication that one relationship held more weight than the other. The silence of the room amplified the drumming in my ears.
